I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

comments.title: Daily Practice Order, Nwyfre Suggestions, Prayer Suggestions, and Physical Culture
comments.content:
Greetings on the evening after Midsummer!

Thank you again for your time and effort in answering these questions - I've found not only your answers to my questions, but reading through past discussions as well, very helpful.

If anyone from the commentariat has thoughts as well, I welcome help from any corner.


  • In what order is it best to do ritual, meditation, divination? From the order of the "Foundations of Magical Practice" series, and the summary at the end of the divination post, I assume the order I listed them above is the way to go, but thought I would check.
    - If the answer is something like "it depends", what kind of factors go into it? Or is this something best learned through experimentation?

  • Do divination results makes for useful meditation themes? Flipped around, is meditation a good way to work through a divinatory reading? If the answer to either is "no", what makes that the case?

  • Any works you particularly like for learning about nwyfre/prana/od/chi/ki/etc?

  • Do you have any thoughts or recommendations for prayer and other non-magical daily spiritual practice for polytheists? I have gotten great use out of you and your wife's Pagan Prayer Beads, and from its recommendation picked up A Book of Pagan Prayer but if you have any other recommendations, I'd love to hear them. I was raised a rather lax Methodist, so I don't have a ton of experience/grounding in ritual or structured prayer.

  • You've mentioned physical culture and its link to occultism a few times - do you have any favorite exercises/programs? I've used Indian Clubs and enjoyed them in the past, but haven't considered them in a spiritual context, as thinking about exercise that way is pretty new to me, so I'd very much appreciate any pointers/guidance. Given that I'm doing your Heathen LBRP and MP exercise everyday, any exercises/programs compatible with that are especially appreciated.



Thanks,
Lavender Impudent Porpoise

replies.title: Re: Daily Practice Order, Nwyfre Suggestions, Prayer Suggestions, and Physical Culture
replies.content:
1) I like to do ritual, meditation, and divination in that order, but your mileage may vary. By all means experiment and find out what works for you.

2) Yes and yes.

3) I'm still waiting for a good book in English on that subject. The best I've seen so far are some of the weirder books in macrobiotic literature, oddly enough, but you've got to know the jargon.

4) Those were the books I was going to recommend. I was raised in a family that was not so much agnostic as apathetic about religion, and I don't know the literature that well.

5) Literally any old-fashioned physical culture exercise is good in this context. Indian clubs are great, so are muscle control exercises (I managed to score a complete set of Maxalding lessons online, which I've worked with extensively) and old-fashioned weightlifting and calisthenics. The Five Rites or Five Tibetans are also good robust exercises that work well with Golden Dawn-style practice.

FWIW I first tried adding the Five Rites in the morning after the CGD Ovate LSRP or LBRP (about an hour later, after a bike ride to drop kids at school) and it was perhaps too much. I had gotten bored of my free weight routine (5 Factor Fitness by Harley Pasternak) and first thought to add it, but divination said that would be too much, so I dropped the weights for now. comments.title: Boundaries for Experimentation in Magical Practice
comments.content:
Another question I thought of that I didn't post earlier:

In many of your responses, you encourage folks to experiment and see what works for them. Any guidelines on what not to experiment with in magical practice? From what I've seen here, it seems like you need to be careful with energy-manipulation techniques and mixing different magical traditions, but any other guidance on what is outside the realm of "try some stuff and see how it goes" would be much appreciated.

Thanks,

replies.title: Re: Boundaries for Experimentation in Magical Practice
replies.content:
I recommend learning one magical system exactly by the book, if you want to get serious about magical training. There is often a little bit of wiggle room in the details, but you want to ask someone experienced where that is.

The places where experimentation tends to blow up are, first, when you're dealing with practices that are intended to move subtle energy through the body, and second, when dealing with spirits. Be very careful about both of those.

replies.title: Re: Boundaries for Experimentation in Magical Practice
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Boundaries for Experimentation in Magical Practice
replies.content: I recommend doing it exactly by the book. Keep invoking your Heathen gods in a religious sense; once you've mastered the CGD system you'll have the background you need to adapt the GD methods to Heathen use, and I know enough people who cross the border between Heathen and Druid paths that you shouldn't have any trouble with the gods in question.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know if you want to banish something like that because it has a place in your psyche and if you nuke it, it could move something else. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Banishing it won't do the trick. You need to understand it and then overwrite it. You understand it by journaling, and figuring out why you've developed that habit -- the reasons are usually far from obvious. You overwrite it by choosing an affirmation and repeating it instead of doing the negative monologue.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've actually had some success with the LBRH, but 'banishing' isn't the right word for what happened.

I concentrated on negative self-talk when banishing the impurities of Mars, invoking Archangel Khamael (Zamael is also traditional). The self-talk was not banished, but the impurities of it were.

What happened, is I became much more sympathetic to my negative self-talk. I appreciated that it wanted to protect me from other people saying the same negative things, with potentially more severe consequences (Mars energy is very violent, but also very protective). The banishing also inspired me to journal by giving my inner-troll a name, and asking it questions, and writing down the first answers that sprang to mind. The result has been that it's criticism have become a lot more conscientious and helpful and it doesn't hold me back from trying new things the way that it used to.

You could try a daily LBRH in addition to the LBRP for a week or so and see if it improves. It's a good ritual for self-development, regardless.

comments.title: Body of Light
comments.content:
Hello John,

Is it true that unless we create the so called "body of light" while still alive our fate will be to suffer the "second death" and lose all our memories and actual being forever, while only an impersonal conscience remains? This idea disturbs me greatly. This would mean our common understanding of reencarnation is wrong and we do not "progress" from one life to the next, only our basest "conscient spark" gets recycled into a completely unralated and random lifetime, plus only people with occult training have any chance. The good, the bad and the most vile of men would all be discarded in the same manner in the end, recycled like empty plastic bottles...

Thanks for taking the time to answer our questions. Peace!

replies.title: Re: Body of Light
replies.content:
That's a highly distorted notion of what happens. Let's go through the basics.

1) Your individuality, the part of you that endures from life to life, is not impersonal. It's who you really are. Your present personality is a projection of your individuality -- it's like a mask (which is what persona means in Latin) that you put on in infancy and take off either when you die or when you achieve enlightenment. People who awaken to their identity as their individuality (that is, who become enlightened) describe the experience as one of delight and immense relief.

2) Each personality is your individuality's latest attempt to create a manifestation for itself on this plane that will express your potential fully, and deal effectively with the karma from previous lives. Each life is the necessary result of your previous lives -- there's nothing random about it -- and all of your lives are part of the maturation process by which your individuality learns the lessons of the human level and prepares to go onward.

3) The second death has nothing to do with the personality. It's the point at which you shed your etheric body, the body of life force. If you don't go through it, you become a ghost -- that is, a soul stuck between lives, unable to go on until someone or something gets you unstuck. It's a miserable fate. Some people do this deliberately, and construct what's called an etheric revenant; they normally do this because they're scared of facing their karma.

4) The term "body of light" gets used to mean quite a variety of different things. In its proper sense it refers to the form of body each of us will have when we finish the human level and go on to bigger and better things. Some people who are in the business of constructing an etheric revenant for themselves claim that they're building the body of light, but that's a whopping misnomer.

5) The body of light is achieved by reaching enliightenment -- that is, awakening to your identity as your individuality in this life: as Zen masters like to say, seeing the face that you had before your mother and father were born. It's not an attempt to dodge karma, it's the completion and fulfillment of your journey through the human level of being.

Does that help clarify things?

replies.title: Re: Body of Light
replies.content:

In reading your responses to the set of questions/concerns expressed by the original poster regarding the Body of Light, I recalled your forward to Mark Stavish's book Between the Gates.

"Between the Gates: Lucid Dreaming, Astral Projection, and the Body of Light in Western Esotericism presents the core theories and practices of the arts of inner journeying and puts them in the context of more than two thousand years of Western esoteric teachings about the subtle body and its role in spiritual development. It's a bravura performance and one that deserves a wide readership among students and practitioners of the world's spiritual traditions."

I don't know if Mr Stavish's book might provide some insight for the original poster. Maybe? Or if might just muddy things... I don't know.

Your point #4 reminded me of Peter Mark Adams' book, The Game of Saturn, wherein the author suggests the Sola Busca Tarocchi encodes a set of rituals intended to empower the subject's intention to return in the same family line ad nauseum, life after life. I love that book. But it's creepy as heck, or indeed hell... For a soul so determined to elude the spiritual momentum he has built up, or to avoid its re balancing... Sounds to me like someone described by your point #4.

Still, aside from that, it would seem that there is indeed work to be done, and we can do it, and the work to be done is upon ourselves. So long as it's the right work...

comments.title: a question that started about writing and then digressed
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

My question is non-magical but I am amenable to solutions that involve magic as well. Though, good magic only (I don't want to insult you by insinuating that you do non-good magic, though knowing nothing of magic I don't know whether that is in fact insulting or just a reasonable precaution to mention, or whatever). I don't have any experience with magic really, just reading a fair bit about it and some experiments with meditations that didn't really seem to result in anything distinctly magical.

I have writer's block, or something like it. I really want to write, and I have written essays and so on during college, some of which I really liked. However, when I try to write now, it seems really hard, and especially when trying to write fiction or anything that is not a diary entry or a tweet / comment like this on the internet or technical stuff for work, I find it impossible to write. No sentence seems to come out well, I don't have any idea of a plot, the story doesn't seem clear in my mind, and the words don't flow. Everything doesn't work. I've read your essay originally posted on The Archdruid Report where you mention that editing brain is different from writing brain, and I've tried using that to decide not to criticize / edit my writing as I write. However, it has not let anything more flow. I don't know if I'm being subconsciously critical / in editing brain or if the problem is something else.

The problem is, when I try to write, nothing comes out, most of the time. I have spent hours wanting to write, even sitting down trying to write, and all I ever managed to write of any particular piece was 4-5 sentences.

The problem seems wide-ranging. It is partly hard to translate whatever I'm saying into words, hard to make a sentence I like, and hard to convey the image I want to convey, or the feeling. But it's also that I don't know what plot I have, or even could have -- so the problem is idea-level too. I often think of writing a story to convey a kind of vibe, or a world from which I can imagine a picture / vibe that I like, or something, but don't know where to go from there.

Actually, having written all this, I feel like one possibility is some problem I have in other areas, in which I have a very limited agency and have a mentality where I must be given the levers I can manipulate before I do anything, and need precisely defined problems and precisely defined ways to solve them, and only then, knowing I have full permission to do what I am doing, try to solve the problem with the prescribed method. I am not sure if this is the same problem as I have with writing, but maybe I am not able to do something without knowing how it will turn out in advance.

Do you have any advice?

replies.title: Re: a question that started about writing and then digressed
replies.content:
Some people don't think in words at all, but think in (for example) diagrams, or in flow-charts and outlines, or in kinetic patterns, or whatever. I'm one of them. As a retired professor, I had to write and lecture for a living, and it was really, really hard at first. But I had to, so I just pushed and pushed and pushed, and after many years I got the trick of creating verbal surrogates for my thought without much effort.

Perhaps you don't naturally think in words, either? One of the ways to tell is if your mind doesn't constantly chatter, but its default setting is some form of wordlessness. (Wordlessness is not at all the same thing as mindlessness.)

replies.title: Re: a question that started about writing and then digressed
replies.content:
Thanks Robert.

I think much of the time I do think in (mentally auditory) words, though I curiously do not remember thinking in words in childhood, only after around 13. Before that I think it may have been images. However when thinking of stories I don't have words, I have a general sense of feeling and image, but that's really hard to translate into words.

Is there any way you can describe what it feels like internally to create verbal surrogates for your thought?

replies.title: Re: a question that started about writing and then digressed
replies.content:
Sure!

I start by putting my finished train of thought in the form of a mental diagram or outline or a detailed table-of-contents; no sentences yet, just labels, which are sometimes in the form of words, but more often as key images, and sometimes colors or smells or rhythms or bell-sounds, that is, sounds of different metals when struck). (Now that I think of it, they are hardly ever as notes or melodies. Most music is as as a sealed book to me. I just can't "grok" it; it does very little for me emotionally or intellectually--the patterns are too uninteresting, sometimes even off-putting.)

Once I've done that much, I can begin to wrestle with the process of verbalization. Every sentence costs me a cconsiderable struggle, with half a dozen or more rewrites, until I finally hammer it into shape and can move on to the next sentence. A relatively short article of about 6-10 pages costs me weeks of effort--writing it is almost as hard as having a tooth pulled, and nearly as painful.

Lecturing is somewhat easier, because I have to keep the words flowing somehow as I talk about each successive point in my outline--but I'm never all that happy with the result. When I could manage it, my students used to get a handout with the outline I had in my head for that lecture or series of lectures. So I carry my audience over the many rough spots in a my lectures by a process that I think of as silent teaching--I don't know how else to describe it--where I communicate wordlessly by inwardly/mentally "exemplifying" what I am talking about externally/verbally. It's probably something like casting a wordless glamour over the space through which we communicate. I can't remember a time when I couldn't do that at need, though age has improved skill.

I've actually written a couple of things that came in between 100 and 200 pages; each of them cost me at least a year's very hard effort or more. That's the hardest thing I know how to do. This brief comment has taken me about an hour to write.

replies.title: Re: a question that started about writing and then digressed
replies.content: Thanks Robert! Your experience of turning thoughts to words is interesting to hear about. I appreciate your taking the time to write this up.

replies.title: Re: a question that started about writing and then digressed
replies.content:
For fifteen minutes a day, make yourself write. No excuses! If you can only write "I can't think of anything I want to write, this is a stupid exercise, why am I wasting my time," and so on, for fifteen minutes, write that. If you can only write gibberish, do that too. Do not let your pen stop moving for fifteen minutes. You are capable of doing this. If you choose not to do it, you may want to explore why.

(You might also want to spend a lot of time thinking and journaling about where you got the mentality you've described. Most people I know who have that sort of blockage got it because they were emotionally or physically abused as children, with "you did it wrong!" as the standard excuse. Digging into those memories and dealing with the emotions connected to them will loosen up a lot of the blockage.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Don't do the divination until you finish the novel. Even if it's not a success, the process of finishing it will teach you an enormous amount about writing, which you will be able to use in future projects. There is always a reason to finish your work. That's one of the most useful pieces of advice I ever got from an editor: always finish what you start.

Once it's finished, cast a house chart. The 1st house will represent self-publishing, the 7th house a commercial publisher, and the 10th house the success you desire. If the 1st house has a mode of perfection with the 10th, self-publish; if the 7th perfects with the 10th, submit it to a commercial publisher; if both do, you've got your choice.

But don't cast that chart until you're finished!

comments.title: Book recommendations

Last week, a mother asked about books to give her 22 year old son as intros to occultism. I only read that comment after monday was over, so would like to add my two cents today:

High Magick by Damien Echols, of the West Memphis Three. A good intro to Golden Dawn techniques, interspersed with the author‘s anecdotes from death row. replies.title: Re: Natural magic for children with night terrord

comments.title: Mundane astrology
comments.content:
Mundane astrology as you've described it looks at the chart for the national capital. Would there be any value in looking at charts for state capitals? Or even specific counties or cities?

Also, you mentioned lunar charts in your ongoing series on Patreon, for new/full moon, lasting for a month. What about daily charts (possibly sunrise or sunset)? Would these have any value?

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology
replies.content: In both cases, you'd have to experiment and find out. I don't know that much has been done along either line.

replies.title: Re: Mundane astrology
replies.content:
Synchronicity alert! A friend of mine has been studying Classical Era Mundane Astrology (for a PhD; not to practice; although she knows I plan to grab everything I can off her and test it myself at some point), which differs quite a bit from what you do, but in that form of astrology the answer is yes on both counts. Anywhere where any kind of decision was to be made could have a chart drafted; if the meeting was regularly scheduled, then it followed the same rules as a national capital, but irregularly convened bodies had their own weird rules. This applied, oddly enough, even if the body in question met less than once a year, as long it's meetings were planned in advance, it would be governed by the standard sets of charts.

As for daily charts, they'd look at them at sunrise, but almost none of them mattered: just as most ingress charts predict business as usual, so to do most daily charts, and so 99% of them are completely irrelevant once the day is done. It was apparently only if the chart interacted heavily with the main charts, and/or was a fairly dramatic chart itself that it would usually be worth more than a cursory read.

comments.title: European occultists and "magic" mushrooms
comments.content:
JMG,

I was reading a book the other day about the cultural history of mushrooms, where the authors noted the "curious" fact that there seems to be no evidence of medieval Europe having known about the psychoactive properties of liberty caps (Psilocybe semilanceata) – curious, because these mushrooms grow widely over much of the continent, are very potent psychedelics (significantly stronger per gram than most psychedelic mushrooms of Latin America, for example) and because European knowledge of medical and psychoactive properties of plants was quite extensive even before the scientific revolution.

If you'd care to speculate for a a little bit on why this might be, I'd love to hear it – one hypothesis I came across on Reddit was that medieval Europeans needed little more than the sight of a relic to have such mind-blowing mystical experiences that they wouldn't care much about the shrooms anyway! – but what I'm really wondering is this:

With your knowledge of occult practice and history, do you think it's at all likely that the properties of liberty caps could have been known by one or more occult schools in Europe before the 1950s, but kept secret and perhaps only talked and written about in very roundabout terms? Is there any way we could know, or will it forever remain speculation?

Thanks as always for hosting this space!

replies.title: Re: European occultists and "magic" mushrooms
replies.content: I remember reading somewhere about 30 years ago that Psilocybe semilanceata was not an indigenous species in Europe and the British Isles at all, but that it had been accidentally introduced there from North America no more than half a century earlier (probably in some imported soil), and had spread rapidly. I have no idea whether this is true, though such things are known to happen with other species of plants and animals. (Does anyone know how to check it out?) But if it is true, then of course Medieval Europeans wouldn't have known about the psychedelic properties of this paticular mushroom.

replies.title: Re: European occultists and "magic" mushrooms

The species was first described scientifically by a Swedish mycologist (Fries) in 1838, so presumably it was fairly widespread in Northern Europe by then (though that might be underestimating Fries' capabilities of globetrotting or his ability to find obscure specimen, or both). I'll see if I can dig up a contemporary mycologist who can help shed some light on the plausibility of the North America import hypothesis.

replies.title: Re: Mutual reception
replies.content: The mutual reception only affects your natal chart -- it doesn't affect the planets in the sky. With that placement, the Moon and Venus both behave as though they're in their rulerships, so they're stronger and more benefic than they would otherwise be, and the aspects in your natal chart function as though both planets were making them -- but only the aspects in your natal chart. Aspects the Moon in the sky makes now are the Moon's business alone.

So, having journaled, meditated and divined I’ve constructeed a ritual patterned after the General approach yet with each element relating to a core theme of one of my major weaknesses.

I have two concerns, the second being the main one but stemming from the first:

1) I had great trouble with the ritual wording, and with trusting that I was reading Ogham well. I had gotten clear Yes/No readings that my chosen theme was a good one to construct a ritual on, but I had to go through several versions of that ritual before I got a favourable 3 card reading on the finished thing. By which time I wondered was I going about it wrong i.e. should I have abandoned the idea altogether at the first unfavourable 3 card reading. Instead I reconsidered, meditated again, thought about it a lot, and reconstructed. Rinse and repeat until I got a ritual whose most notable quality was it was far more bluntly worded than earlier versions – no prevarication or qualification...just “bless me with X, and banish Y”.

As painful a process as it was, my sense was that I’d hit on something workable. Does this seem like a foolish way to have proceeded?

2) Having reached something I thought worked, and begun performing, I have I think noticed some subtle but positive results. However I was assailed abruptly by hesitation about the wording of one of the banishings.

To avoid giving detail on an ongoing working I’ll give an alternative example. Chapter 5 advises carefully chosen intention, and expression, to avoid unwanted results. Let’s imagine the thing being banished was a habit of “hesitancy”. Language can have so many different inflexions. Entrenched, reflexive hesitancy would be a debilitating thing. But my worry is that to simply banish all “hesitancy” could get rid of useful hesitancy i.e. necessary caution.

It comes down to this: having meditated and journaled on it I know inwardly that in banishing quality “X” I am referring to THAT quality which I can see expressing itself in so many of my behaviours, but the words I seem to have been guided to use by Ogham do not have any nuance.

I have ended up concluding that the blunt expression in the ritual is partly why it ought be effective, and that I should trust it, and furthermore I think this very nit-picking trouble and fear I’m having is a way the quality I’m trying to banish has of fighting back.

I hope this makes sense: I guess I’m just hoping you can either reassure me, or if alarm bells are ringing about what I’m doing to let me know.

Sorry for the length, and thank you so much for any advice.

2) Ignore your second thoughts. They're efforts on the part of your subconscious to hold onto its familiar dysfunctions. Hesitation and nitpicking are a common way this happens. Just keep on doing the ritual and affirming your intention.
